explanation_md: `g_val` is a global variable initialized to 5. Each call to `increment` adds 1 to it. After two calls, `g_val` is 7. The output is `7`. Because globals live for the entire program, `increment` modifies the same storage both times.

prompt_md:
What value does an uninitialized global `int` variable have in C++?

- a. Indeterminate (undefined behavior to read it).
- b. Zero — global variables are zero-initialized before `main` runs.
- c. 1 — the compiler always starts counters at 1.
- d. Whatever value happens to be at that memory address.

options.b: Zero — global variables are zero-initialized before `main` runs.
options.c: 1 — the compiler always starts counters at 1.
options.d: Whatever value happens to be at that memory address.

explanation_md: The C++ standard guarantees that variables with static storage duration (which includes all globals) are zero-initialized before any other initialization. An `int` global with no explicit initializer is `0`, not indeterminate. This is different from local variables, which are indeterminate if not initialized.
The local variable `g_x { 20 }` in `main` shadows the global `g_x { 10 }`. Inside `main`, the name `g_x` refers to the local. The program prints `20`. The global is hidden but not modified.

prompt_md:
Where must a global variable be defined in a C++ source file?

- a. Inside `main()`, before any other statement.
- b. Inside a namespace block.
- c. Outside all function definitions, at file scope.
- d. At the very top of the file, before any `#include` directives.

options.c: Outside all function definitions, at file scope.
options.d: At the very top of the file, before any `#include` directives.

explanation_md: A global variable is defined at file scope — outside any function. It does not have to be first in the file (it can appear after `#include` lines or other declarations), and it does not have to be inside a namespace (though it may be). The key requirement is that it is not nested inside any function body.

prompt_md:
Why is the `g_` prefix convention used for global variables?

- a. It is required by the C++ standard for global variables.
- b. It prevents the variable from being modified inside functions.
- c. It makes the global status visible at the use site, so readers know the variable is not local.
- d. It tells the linker that the variable should not be exported.

options.a: It is required by the C++ standard for global variables.
options.b: It prevents the variable from being modified inside functions.
options.c: It makes the global status visible at the use site, so readers know the variable is not local.
options.d: It tells the linker that the variable should not be exported.

explanation_md: The `g_` prefix is a naming convention, not a language requirement. Its purpose is readability: when you see `g_maxScore` inside a function, you immediately know it is a global and not a parameter or local variable. The prefix has no effect on the variable's accessibility, constness, or linkage.
